_meta.artifact: Copywriting framework library + power words + DR register map
_meta.description: App-wide read-only seed data for Choir. Frameworks + their verbatim LLM prompts are the active ingredient for the post-generation engine. Power words feed the headline/hook generator. dr_register_map governs which DR persuasion techniques are admissible per content register (editorial vs ads/sales).
extraction_notes: 13 named frameworks extracted verbatim. PAIPS (the LinkedIn-native framework) and the visual reference table list it, but the source provides NO ready-to-use ChatGPT prompt for PAIPS (only its 5-part structure). llm_prompt for PAIPS is null with a note — do not invent one.

Frameworks 1-12 (AIDA through Consistent Contrasting) each carry a VERBATIM ChatGPT prompt from the source fenced code blocks (linkedin-copywriting-rules.md lines 238-424). Extracted character-for-character including [YOUR TOPIC] / [YOUR AUDIENCE] placeholders.

Source numbering quirk preserved: the source's '10 Copywriting Frameworks' section actually numbers items 1-12 (AIDA=1 ... Consistent Contrasting=12) plus PAIPS as a separate preceding section = 13 named frameworks total.
100 power words = exactly 20 per category x 5 categories, verbatim from lines 506-519.

dr_register_map columns extracted verbatim from 05-marketing-demand/copywriting/_index.md section 2 (lines 49-64). The 11 gate-matched urgency phrases are the deterministically-enforced subset (anti-slop-universal.md lines 220-230) — these hard-fail ONLY under --register editorial.

frameworks.name: PAIPS
frameworks.full_name: Problem, Agitate, Intrigue, Paint a Positive Future, Present Your Solution
frameworks.description: A LinkedIn-specific copywriting framework. Moves the reader from a painful problem, deepens it, opens an intrigue loop, paints the better future, then delivers the solution.
frameworks.when_to_use: LinkedIn-native content (general-purpose default framework for the platform).
structure: P -- Problem: First line highlights a painful problem your target audience experiences. It must elicit a strong emotion and compel the reader to the next line.
A -- Agitate: Expand on the pain/problem by detailing it. Make the reader feel the full weight of the problem. Describe the consequences, the frustration, the cost of inaction.
I -- Intrigue: Introduce something intriguing that makes the audience think 'wait, what's that?' Create an open loop that must be resolved.
P -- Paint a Positive Future: Show what life looks like after the problem is solved. Make the reader visualize the better outcome. Paint it in specific, sensory terms.
S -- Present Your Solution: Deliver the solution. This is where you provide the framework, steps, tool, or approach that resolves the problem established in the first line.
frameworks.llm_prompt_note: Source provides PAIPS structure only (linkedin-copywriting-rules.md lines 203-221) with NO ready-to-use ChatGPT prompt. Engineers: either author one mirroring the 12 prompts that DO exist, or omit PAIPS from the prompt-driven generator. Do not fabricate as if it came from the KB.

frameworks.name: AIDA
frameworks.description: Grab attention, build interest with details, create desire for the outcome, drive action.
frameworks.when_to_use: Product launches, offer posts, lead magnet promotions.
structure: Attention: Bold hook that stops the scroll
Interest: Expand with relevant details or data
Desire: Show the transformation or benefit
Action: Clear CTA
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the AIDA framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: Start with an attention-grabbing hook, build interest with specific details, create desire by showing the transformation, end with a clear call to action.
Keep sentences under 12 words. Use whitespace between lines.

frameworks.name: ACCA
frameworks.description: Make the reader aware of a problem, help them comprehend its impact, build conviction in your solution, drive action.
frameworks.when_to_use: Educational content, thought leadership, methodology posts.
structure: Awareness: Surface a problem or gap
Comprehension: Explain why it matters
Conviction: Provide evidence or logic for your approach
Action: Tell them what to do next
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the ACCA framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: Start by making the reader aware of a key problem, help them comprehend its full impact, build conviction with evidence or logic, end with a clear action step.
Keep it concise and scannable.

frameworks.name: BAB
frameworks.description: Show the current painful state, show the desired future state, bridge with your solution.
frameworks.when_to_use: Transformation stories, case studies, service/product showcases.
structure: Before: Paint the current painful reality
After: Paint the desired outcome
Bridge: Show how to get from Before to After
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the BAB (Before-After-Bridge) framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: Describe the painful current state (Before), paint the ideal future (After), then bridge the gap with a clear solution or approach.
Use short, punchy sentences.

frameworks.name: CC
frameworks.description: Strip all fluff. Say what matters in as few words as possible.
frameworks.when_to_use: Hot takes, opinion posts, quote-style posts.
structure: State the point directly
Support with 1-2 lines
Close with conviction
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the Clear and Concise framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: Make your point in the first line, support it with 1-2 short supporting statements, close with conviction. Maximum 5-7 lines total. No filler.

frameworks.name: FAB
frameworks.description: State what it does (feature), why that matters (advantage), what it means for the reader (benefit).
frameworks.when_to_use: Product/tool recommendations, feature announcements, comparison posts.
structure: Feature: What it is/does
Advantage: Why that capability matters
Benefit: How it improves the reader's life/work
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the FAB (Features-Advantages-Benefits) framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: Start with a key feature, explain the advantage it provides, then translate into a concrete benefit for the reader. Repeat for 2-3 features.

frameworks.name: FE
frameworks.description: Acknowledge a shared frustration, then provide encouragement and a path forward.
frameworks.when_to_use: Motivational content, community building, relatable posts.
structure: Frustration: Validate the reader's struggle
Encouragement: Provide hope and direction
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the Frustration-Encouragement framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: Open by acknowledging a real frustration your audience faces. Validate their struggle. Then shift to encouragement with practical hope and direction.

frameworks.name: PPF
frameworks.description: Share the past situation, describe the present state, project the future trajectory.
frameworks.when_to_use: Journey posts, industry trend analysis, personal growth narratives.
structure: Past: Where things were
Present: Where things are now
Future: Where things are heading
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the PPF (Past-Present-Future) framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: Start with how things used to be (Past), describe the current state (Present), then paint where things are heading (Future). Use specific details for each time period.

frameworks.name: PAS
frameworks.description: State the problem, agitate it by deepening the pain, deliver the solution.
frameworks.when_to_use: Pain-point content, educational posts, product/service posts.
structure: Problem: Name the specific problem
Agitate: Twist the knife -- show consequences, emotional toll, cascading effects
Solution: Provide the resolution
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the PAS (Problem-Agitate-Solution) framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: State a specific problem your audience faces, agitate it by detailing the consequences and emotional impact, then deliver a clear solution.

frameworks.name: PASTOR
frameworks.full_name: Problem, Amplify, Story, Transformation, Offer, Response
frameworks.description: A comprehensive narrative framework that moves through the full persuasion arc.
frameworks.when_to_use: Long-form posts, sales-oriented content, case study posts.
structure: Problem: Identify the pain
Amplify: Show what happens if the problem persists
Story: Share a relevant narrative
Transformation: Show the before/after
Offer: Present your solution
Response: Ask for action
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the PASTOR framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: Identify a key problem (Problem), amplify the consequences (Amplify), share a relevant story (Story), show the transformation (Transformation), present your solution (Offer), ask for a response (Response).

frameworks.name: PPPP
frameworks.description: Make a promise, paint a picture of the outcome, prove it with evidence, push toward action.
frameworks.when_to_use: Offer posts, authority-building content, results posts.
structure: Promise: State the outcome the reader will get
Picture: Help them visualize achieving it
Proof: Back it up with evidence, data, or testimonials
Push: Drive them to act
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the 4P (Promise-Picture-Proof-Push) framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: Open with a bold promise, paint a vivid picture of the outcome, provide proof through data or examples, then push toward a specific action.

frameworks.name: Friend-Expert
frameworks.description: Position yourself as both a trustworthy friend AND a knowledgeable expert. The friend component builds approachability; the expert component establishes authority.
frameworks.when_to_use: Consultative sales content, service positioning, trust-building posts, personal brand posts.
structure: Friend: Be approachable, relatable, trustworthy -- speak like a peer who genuinely cares
Expert: Demonstrate deep knowledge, provide real solutions backed by experience
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the Friend-Expert framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: Open with a relatable, approachable tone (like a friend sharing advice), then transition to authoritative insight with specific expertise and solutions. Balance warmth with credibility throughout.

frameworks.name: Consistent Contrasting
frameworks.description: Highlight what makes you aligned with your audience's needs (consistent) while differentiating from competitors or conventional wisdom (contrasting).
frameworks.when_to_use: Competitive differentiation, comparison content, 'why us' messaging, positioning posts.
structure: Consistent: Show alignment with what the customer values, needs, or expects
Contrasting: Differentiate from alternatives -- what you do differently and why it matters
frameworks.llm_prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post using the Consistent Contrasting framework.
Topic: [YOUR TOPIC]
Target audience: [YOUR AUDIENCE]
Structure: First establish common ground with what the audience values (consistent), then sharply differentiate your approach from the norm or competitors (contrasting). Make the contrast specific and evidence-based.

power_words._description: 100 captivating words that trigger stronger emotional responses in readers, organized by function. Exactly 20 per category. Feeds the headline/hook/wording generator. Source: linkedin-copywriting-rules.md:502-519 (from 100_captivating_words.jpeg, Notion export).
power_words._warning: Power words OVERLAP heavily with the editorial kill-list. 'Shocking' appears in BOTH attention_grabbers and emotional_appeal (the source lists it twice; total distinct words < 100). Many entries (Revolutionary, Ultimate, Game-Changing, World-Class, Breakthrough, Disruptive, Groundbreaking, Best/Leading-adjacent) are register-GATED: legitimate for ads/sales/LinkedIn-hooks, but HARD-FAIL under editorial register via sanitize-copy.mjs. The generator MUST consult dr_register_map.register_gated_phrases + anti-slop kill-list before surfacing power words for editorial-register output.

dr_register_map._description: The DR Register Map — the firewall doctrine that governs which direct-response persuasion techniques are admissible per content register. register_neutral techniques are safe in ANY register including editorial. register_gated techniques convert on paid ads / long-form sales letters but POISON an editorial website page (suppressed/hard-failed under --register editorial). Source: 05-marketing-demand/copywriting/_index.md section 2 (lines 46-65).

editorial._label: register_neutral — import into ANY register, INCLUDING editorial. This IS the editorial-admissible column.
techniques: Specificity / exact figures (cut onboarding from six weeks to four days)
Clarity: one idea per sentence, plain words
Honest, counted social proof (52 nations, not 'the world over')

ads_sales._label: register_gated — ads + long-form sales ONLY, suppressed for editorial. Available in addition to the neutral column when register is ads/sales.
techniques: Personality-forward pitchman voice (permitted ONLY on a first-person-founder /about page)

register_gated_phrases._description: The 11 manufactured-urgency/scarcity CTA phrases that are DETERMINISTICALLY enforced. sanitize-copy.mjs hard-fails (exit 1) on these ONLY under --register editorial, matched on word boundaries (so 'act now' never fires inside 'react now'), and stays SILENT for ads/sales/default register. This is the code-enforced half of the DR Register Map. Engineers: replicate this gate in Choir's copy-quality pipeline for editorial-register output. Source: anti-slop-universal.md:220-230 (canonical, gate-matched).

slop_structural_tells._description: S1-S7 structural slop tells from alive-without-slop.md. S1-S5 are DETERMINISTICALLY enforced (hard-fail under --register editorial via sanitize-copy.mjs Pattern 11). S6-S7 are taste-level (hostile-editor critic only, no code detector). These are the structural complement to the kill-list vocabulary — copy can read clean of banned WORDS and still trip these. Source: alive-without-slop.md:59-69.

deterministic_S1_S5.definition: A noun equated to a noun for portentous effect
X, not Y / not just Z / is never Y / rather than / 'X isn't Y. It's Z.'
deterministic_S1_S5.killed_example: Posts in your voice, not a ghostwriter's.
Outbound that survives the AI flood.
Bring your setup. Leave with a plan.

deterministic_S1_S5.definition: Spelled cardinals in a short headline

advisory_S6_S7.killed_example: Ask any engineer: read access only.

advisory_S6_S7.definition: Short, witty, rhythmically balanced; reads like a slogan

alive_craft_doctrine._description: The four sources of life in copy (none of them rhetoric) — the positive craft register that solves both AI-slop AND flat-after-de-slopping. Used to steer the generator toward ALIVE output, not just to filter slop. Source: alive-without-slop.md:20-51.
alive_craft_doctrine.governing_test: The dinner test: Would the author SAY this sentence out loud to a peer at a dinner — or is it written-for-a-website? If the latter, cut it. A slightly long, plain, specific sentence beats a short clever one every time. Punchy is the defect, not the merit.

four_sources_of_life.source: Specificity, to the point of being checkable (Hopkins)
four_sources_of_life.test: Could a competitor paste this exact sentence onto their own site? If yes, it's a platitude — cut it.
four_sources_of_life.de_slop_direction: When a line goes flat, go MORE specific, not blander.

four_sources_of_life.source: Reason-why — the mechanism is the interesting part (Hopkins / Schwartz)
four_sources_of_life.test: For every claim, can you write the next sentence starting with 'because' or 'here's how'? If not, you have a slogan, not a claim. Delete it.
four_sources_of_life.de_slop_direction: The mechanism IS the headline; the promise is the subhead.

four_sources_of_life.source: Demonstration — put the reader inside one true scene (Caples / Collier)
four_sources_of_life.test: Convert every abstract benefit into a concrete moment the reader steps into. An adjective labels; a scene is lived.
four_sources_of_life.de_slop_direction: A real scene is neither a dry spec nor a hollow zinger.

four_sources_of_life.test: If a line is doing rhetorical work to SEEM exciting, it's almost always not aimed at a real desire. Name the ONE strongest want the buyer already has, and point the plain sentence at that.
four_sources_of_life.de_slop_direction: Each line earns the next with 'which means', 'the reason for that', 'here's how' — gradualization gives flat copy direction.

alive_craft_doctrine.fifth_governing_principle: Respect the reader (Ogilvy): 'The consumer is not a moron; she is your wife.' Assume intelligence, explain depth without condescending, never mansplain ('Here's why 👇'), never over-explain the obvious.
